1: bayom hahu yihyeh maqor niftach leveit David uleyoshevei Yerushalayim lechatat uleNidah: ‘On that day a fountain will be opened for the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem to cleanse them from sin and impurity.’ Maqor (fountain/spring — a natural, flowing, perpetual source of water) contrasts with the cisterns and wells of the OT: a fountain is never depleted, fed by underground springs. Chatat (sin-offering/sin) and nidah (impurity — the ritual uncleanness of menstruation, used more broadly for severe uncleanness) define the two categories of defilement that the fountain cleanses: moral guilt and ritual uncleanness together. The opened fountain is the OT’s image for the inexhaustible cleansing that the NT identifies as the blood of Christ: “the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin” (1 John 1:7). John 7:38 (rivers of living water flowing from the believer) and Revelation 22:1 (the river of the water of life, clear as crystal) develop the fountain imagery further.
2: vehayah bayom hahu ne’um YHWH tzvaot akrit et shemot ha’atzabim min ha’aretz velo yizzachru od: ‘On that day, declares the LORD of Hosts, I will remove the names of the idols from the land, so that they will no longer be remembered.’ The removal of the shemot ha’atzabim (names of the idols) is more thorough than the removal of the physical idols: when the name is forgotten, the idol has no residual claim on memory or worship. The OT’s concern for the names of false gods (Psalms 16:4: I will not take their names on my lips; Exodus 23:13: make no mention of the names of other gods) becomes the eschatological promise that the names themselves will be cut off. The NT equivalent: all names and powers subjected to Christ, so that only the name of Jesus retains ultimate honor (Philippians 2:9-11: the name above every name).
3: The severity of the anti-prophecy law in the eschatological period: parents themselves pronounce the death sentence on a false prophet child. The parental judgment (deqaruhu aviv ve’immo, his father and mother who bore him will pierce/stab him) uses the same verb daqqar (pierce/stab) as Zechariah 12:10 (asher daqaru, whom they pierced) — the only two occurrences of this verb in Zechariah, creating a verbal link between the piercing of the shepherd-king and the piercing of false prophets. The eschatological community so values true prophecy over false that even family loyalty yields to prophetic accountability.
4: vehayah bayom hahu yevoshu hanevi’im ish mecheziyonato behinnabe’o velo yilbeshu aderet se’ar lema’an kachassh: ‘On that day every prophet will be ashamed of his prophetic vision, and they will no longer put on a hairy cloak in order to deceive.’ The hairy cloak (adderet se’ar) was the distinctive garment of prophets — Elijah’s mantle (2 Kings 1:8: a man wearing a garment of hair), John the Baptist’s clothing (Matthew 3:4: his clothing was camel’s hair). The false prophet who disguises himself in prophetic clothing is the OT precursor to Jesus’s warning about false prophets who come in sheep’s clothing (Matthew 7:15) — the external prophetic costume used to claim an authority that is not authentic. In the eschatological era, the hairy cloak will no longer be used deceptively because the era of false prophecy is ended.
5: ve’amar lo navi anochi ki ish oved adamah anochi ki adam hiqnani mineurai: ‘He will say, “I am not a prophet. I am a farmer — someone has owned me as a servant since my youth.”’ The former false prophet’s disavowal of prophetic identity — claiming to be a mere farmer-servant — is the prophetic silence that marks the end of the prophetic era. The insistence on being ish oved adamah (a man who works the ground/a farmer) echoes Amos 7:14 (I am not a prophet, nor a prophet’s son, but a herdsman) — Amos’s authentic rejection of the prophetic title in a context where he nevertheless prophesied. The false prophet’s claim to be merely a servant-farmer inverts Amos: Amos genuinely was a farmer but spoke truly; the false prophet genuinely was a “prophet” but now denies it to escape accountability.
6: ve’amar elav mah hamakkot ha’eleh bein yadeycha ve’amar asher hukkeiti beit me’ahavai: ‘When someone asks him, “What are these wounds on your hands?” he will answer, “Wounds I received in the house of my friends/lovers.”’ The wounds on the hands (makkot ... bein yadeycha) of the former false prophet — apparently from ecstatic prophetic self-wounding (1 Kings 18:28: the Baal prophets cut themselves) — betray his prophetic past even when he denies it. His cover story (“received in the house of my friends”) is inadequate: the wounds on the hands are the mark of the ecstatic prophet. The NT has applied Zechariah 13:6 to Christ’s nail-wounds — “wounds in the house of my friends/lovers” becoming Christ’s wounds inflicted by those he loved — though this reading requires reading against the original context (where the speaker is a false prophet).
7: cherev uri al ro’i ve’al gever amiti ne’um YHWH tzvaot hach et haro’i tefutzeyna hatzon: ‘Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who is my associate, declares the LORD of Hosts. Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered.’ The most theologically dense verse in Zechariah 9-14. Two key terms: (1) ro’i (my shepherd — YHWH’s own shepherd, the one he sent and possesses); (2) gever amiti (the man who is my associate/fellow/equal — from ‘amith, a word meaning “fellow citizen, associate, one who shares one’s standing”). The shepherd is not merely YHWH’s servant but YHWH’s amith — a term of equality, not subordination. YHWH commands the sword against his own equal/associate — an act of incomprehensible self-sacrifice. Jesus cites this verse in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:31): “You will all fall away because of me this night, for it is written, I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.” The scattering of the disciples is the NT fulfillment of the sheep-scattering that Zechariah predicted.
8: vehayah lechol ha’aretz ne’um YHWH shnei chelakim bah yikaretu yigve’u uhashelishi yivvater bah: ‘Throughout the whole land, declares the LORD, two thirds shall be cut off and die, but one third will be left.’ The judgment proportions — two thirds destroyed, one third surviving — are the OT’s most explicit statement of the remnant theology: judgment is not total destruction but pruning that leaves a faithful remainder. Ezekiel 5:1-12 uses a similar tripartite division of the hair (one third burned, one third struck with the sword, one third scattered to the wind). The survival of one third is not accidental or a concession to human resilience but YHWH’s deliberate preservation of a covenant remnant through the purifying judgment.
9: veheveti et hashelishit ba’esh utzaraptim katzeref et hakesef uvechantem kivalchan et hazahav hu yiqra bishmi va’ani e’eneh oto amarti ammi hu vehu yomar YHWH Elohai: ‘I will put that third through the fire and refine them as silver is refined, and test them as gold is tested. They will call on my name and I will answer them; I will say, “They are my people,” and they will say, “The LORD is my God.”’ The refining metaphor — fire as the purifying agent that removes dross from silver and impurities from gold — is the OT’s standard image for covenantal purification through suffering (Isaiah 48:10; Malachi 3:2-3; Psalms 66:10). The result of the refining is the restoration of the covenant formula: ammi hu (they are my people) / YHWH Elohai (the LORD is my God). This formula, originally given at Sinai (Leviticus 26:12), broken by the exile (Hosea 1:9: Lo-ammi = not my people), and now restored through the fire of refinement, is the covenant relationship fully recovered. The NT applies this restoration to the church: “once you were not a people, but now you are God’s people” (1 Peter 2:10).
